quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 6, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Counting Ions and Atoms from Mass
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Converting Mass to Number of Ions/Atoms, Molar Mass Calculations, Avogadro's Number Applications, Counting Atoms in Compounds

questions.question: A student dissolves 2.84 g of sodium chloride (NaCl) in water. What is the number of chloride ions present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of NaCl

Molar mass NaCl = 58.44 g/mol
Moles NaCl = 2.84 / 58.44 = 0.0486 mol

Step 2: Find moles of Cl⁻

Each NaCl gives 1 Cl⁻ ion
Moles Cl⁻ = 0.0486 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of Cl⁻ ions

Number = 0.0486 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $2.93 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 4.40 g of carbon dioxide (CO₂) is released during a reaction. How many oxygen atoms does this amount contain?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of CO₂

Molar mass CO₂ = 44.01 g/mol
Moles CO₂ = 4.40 / 44.01 = 0.1000 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each CO₂ has 2 O atoms
Moles O = 0.1000 × 2 = 0.2000 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.2000 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.20 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: A chemist weighs 9.80 g of sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄). Determine the number of hydrogen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of H₂SO₄

Molar mass H₂SO₄ = 98.08 g/mol
Moles H₂SO₄ = 9.80 / 98.08 = 0.100 mol

Step 2: Find moles of H atoms

Each H₂SO₄ has 2 H atoms
Moles H = 0.100 × 2 = 0.200 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of H atoms

Number = 0.200 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.20 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 1.60 g of methane (CH₄) is burned in air. How many hydrogen atoms are involved?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of CH₄

Molar mass CH₄ = 16.04 g/mol
Moles CH₄ = 1.60 / 16.04 = 0.100 mol

Step 2: Find moles of H atoms

Each CH₄ has 4 H atoms
Moles H = 0.100 × 4 = 0.400 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of H atoms

Number = 0.400 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $2.41 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 5.85 g of sodium nitrate (NaNO₃) is used in an experiment. Find the number of oxygen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of NaNO₃

Molar mass NaNO₃ = 85.00 g/mol
Moles NaNO₃ = 5.85 / 85.00 = 0.0688 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each NaNO₃ has 3 O atoms
Moles O = 0.0688 × 3 = 0.2064 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.2064 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.24 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: A sample contains 3.40 g of ammonia (NH₃). Calculate the number of nitrogen atoms in the sample.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of NH₃

Molar mass NH₃ = 17.03 g/mol
Moles NH₃ = 3.40 / 17.03 = 0.200 mol

Step 2: Find moles of N atoms

Each NH₃ has 1 N atom
Moles N = 0.200 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of N atoms

Number = 0.200 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.20 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 12.0 g of magnesium oxide (MgO) is heated strongly. How many oxide ions does this correspond to?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of MgO

Molar mass MgO = 40.30 g/mol
Moles MgO = 12.0 / 40.30 = 0.2978 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O²⁻ ions

Each MgO has 1 O²⁻ ion
Moles O²⁻ = 0.2978 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O²⁻ ions

Number = 0.2978 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.79 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: A lab technician measures 6.30 g of nitric acid (HNO₃). Determine the number of oxygen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of HNO₃

Molar mass HNO₃ = 63.02 g/mol
Moles HNO₃ = 6.30 / 63.02 = 0.1000 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each HNO₃ has 3 O atoms
Moles O = 0.1000 × 3 = 0.3000 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.3000 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.81 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 7.10 g of calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) decomposes on heating. How many carbon atoms are present initially?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of CaCO₃

Molar mass CaCO₃ = 100.09 g/mol
Moles CaCO₃ = 7.10 / 100.09 = 0.0709 mol

Step 2: Find moles of C atoms

Each CaCO₃ has 1 C atom
Moles C = 0.0709 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of C atoms

Number = 0.0709 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $4.27 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: A beaker contains 1.80 g of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆). Calculate the number of oxygen atoms present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of glucose

Molar mass C₆H₁₂O₆ = 180.16 g/mol
Moles glucose = 1.80 / 180.16 = 0.0100 mol

Step 2: Find moles of O atoms

Each glucose has 6 O atoms
Moles O = 0.0100 × 6 = 0.0600 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of O atoms

Number = 0.0600 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $3.61 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 8.00 g of potassium chloride (KCl) is dissolved in water. How many potassium ions are present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of KCl

Molar mass KCl = 74.55 g/mol
Moles KCl = 8.00 / 74.55 = 0.1073 mol

Step 2: Find moles of K⁺ ions

Each KCl gives 1 K⁺ ion
Moles K⁺ = 0.1073 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of K⁺ ions

Number = 0.1073 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $6.46 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: A student adds 2.30 g of ethanol (C₂H₅OH) to a solution. What is the number of hydrogen atoms added?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of ethanol

Molar mass C₂H₅OH = 46.07 g/mol
Moles ethanol = 2.30 / 46.07 = 0.0500 mol

Step 2: Find moles of H atoms

Each ethanol has 6 H atoms
Moles H = 0.0500 × 6 = 0.300 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of H atoms

Number = 0.300 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.81 \times 10^{23}$

questions.question: 14.2 g of aluminum sulfate (Al₂(SO₄)₃) is prepared. Find the number of sulfate ions present.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of Al₂(SO₄)₃

Molar mass Al₂(SO₄)₃ = 342.14 g/mol
Moles = 14.2 / 342.14 = 0.0415 mol

Step 2: Find moles of SO₄²⁻ ions

Each formula unit has 3 SO₄²⁻ ions
Moles SO₄²⁻ = 0.0415 × 3 = 0.1245 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of SO₄²⁻ ions

Number = 0.1245 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $7.50 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: A sample contains 4.95 g of phosphorus pentachloride (PCl₅). How many chlorine atoms are present?
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of PCl₅

Molar mass PCl₅ = 208.22 g/mol
Moles = 4.95 / 208.22 = 0.0238 mol

Step 2: Find moles of Cl atoms

Each PCl₅ has 5 Cl atoms
Moles Cl = 0.0238 × 5 = 0.119 mol

Step 3: Calculate number of Cl atoms

Number = 0.119 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $7.16 \times 10^{22}$

questions.question: 0.88 g of helium gas (He) is placed in a balloon. Determine the number of helium atoms.
questions.explanation:
Step 1: Calculate moles of He

Molar mass He = 4.00 g/mol
Moles He = 0.88 / 4.00 = 0.22 mol

Step 2: Calculate number of He atoms

Number = 0.22 × $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ = $1.32 \times 10^{23}$

scoreMessages.90: Outstanding! You've mastered counting ions and atoms from mass! 🧪🔬
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the steps: mass → moles → particles.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on calculating molar mass correctly and identifying atom ratios.
scoreMessages.0: Please review molar mass calculations, mole concept, and Avogadro's number applications.
